page: Vultr voc-g-96c-256gb-1280s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute instance powered by the x86_64 AMD EPYC-Rome processor architecture. The system features 96 physical cores and 96 virtual CPUs running at a base clock speed of 2.0 GHz, with a single thread per core configuration. It is equipped with 256.0 GB of system memory, providing approximately 2.67 GB of RAM per core. Storage is bundled with 1280 GB of local NVMe SSD capacity.

Benchmark data indicates strong computational capabilities. In stress-ng single-core CPU performance testing, the server achieved a score of 2460.86, placing it in the top 25% of tested instances. Multi-core performance is highly competitive, with a stress-ng multi-core score of 213580.47, which ranks in the top 10% tier. The CPU allocation is shared, which may introduce performance variability under sustained multi-tenant workloads, representing a potential tradeoff for continuous high-utilization tasks.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, this instance offers high resource density, combining a massive core count with fast NVMe storage at a shared-CPU tier. This makes it a viable option for parallelizable workloads that require substantial compute capacity without the premium cost of dedicated hardware. Recommended workloads include large-scale web serving, batch processing, data compilation, and distributed database nodes that benefit from high multi-core throughput and local NVMe storage.
description: Vultr voc-g-96c-256gb-1280s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute server featuring 96 AMD EPYC-Rome physical cores on the x86_64 architecture, running at 2.0 GHz with a single thread per core. The instance is configured with 256.0 GB of RAM, yielding 2.67 GB per core, and includes 1280 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. In benchmark evaluations, the server demonstrates strong single-core performance with a stress-ng score of 2460.86 and top-tier multi-core performance with a score of 213580.47. Although the shared CPU allocation introduces potential resource contention tradeoffs, the high core density and fast local storage make this instance highly cost-efficient for parallel processing. It is well-suited for multi-threaded workloads, batch processing, and large-scale web serving.
